summary:
The Mahajanapadas ('great kingdoms,' c. 600–300 BC) were the sixteen major political entities of northern India during the late Vedic and early Buddhist periods — a constellation of kingdoms and oligarchic republics across the Gangetic plain and northwest India that represented the most politically sophisticated societies in the Indian subcontinent before the Maurya Empire. They ranged from large monarchies like Magadha, Kosala, and Avanti to oligarchic republics (gana-sanghas) like the Vajji confederation and the Shakya clan (home of the Buddha).

The period of the Mahajanapadas saw accelerating urbanization along the Ganges, the rise of a money economy (use of punchmark coins), increasing agricultural productivity from iron tools, and the flourishing of religious innovation — both [[event:1960|Buddha Siddhartha Gautama]] and [[event:1961|Mahavira and Jainism]] lived and taught in this context of political competition and religious questioning. The progressive absorption of smaller states by larger ones during this period — Magadha ultimately conquering most rivals — created the political consolidation that would eventually enable Chandragupta Maurya to build an empire. The oligarchic republics, with their traditions of collective decision-making in assemblies, have been noted as indigenous Indian precedents for democratic governance.
